Step 1: Recall Cystic Artery Origin  
– The cystic artery most commonly branches from the right hepatic artery.  
– It typically courses through Calot’s (hepatobiliary) triangle, in which the boundaries are:  
  • Cystic duct  
  • Common hepatic duct  
  • Inferior liver edge

Step 2: Identify Junction for Ligation  
– During cholecystectomy, the cystic artery is ligated close to its origin to minimize bleeding.  
– Its origin is the point where it branches off the right hepatic artery.

Step 3: Match to Answer Choices  
(A) Gastroduodenal – gives off superior pancreaticoduodenal, not cystic artery  
(B) Left gastric – supplies lesser curvature of stomach  
(C) Left hepatic – gives branches to left lobe of liver  
(D) Right gastric – runs along lesser curvature  
(E) Right hepatic – gives off the cystic artery  

Final Answer: \boxed{Right\ hepatic\ artery} (E)

Step 1: Recognize the clinical scenario—this patient had an acute episode of oliguric renal failure 6 weeks before death, with evidence (granular casts, elevated BUN/Cr) pointing to acute tubular necrosis (ATN) from ischemic injury.

Step 2: Recall the phases of ischemic ATN  
  • Initiation phase (cellular injury, 0–36 hr)  
  • Maintenance (oliguric) phase (up to ~1–2 weeks): low urine output, elevated BUN/Cr  
  • Recovery (polyuric) phase (days to weeks): epithelial cell regeneration, rising urine output, falling BUN/Cr  

Step 3: Correlate with the timeline  
  • 5 weeks ago: patient was in maintenance phase with BUN 40, Cr 3.5, granular casts  
  • 4 weeks ago: oliguria resolved and BUN/Cr returned to normal—he entered recovery and had functional restoration  
  • Now (6 weeks out): two weeks into recovery, histologic repair of tubular epithelium is essentially complete  

Step 4: Know the histologic outcome of fully recovered ATN  
  If basement membranes remain intact, tubular epithelial cells regenerate and restore normal architecture without fibrosis or scarring.

Answer choice review  
  (A) Acute inflammation—seen early in injury, not after full recovery  
  (B) Fibrinous exudate—characteristic of severe glomerular injury or serous surfaces, not in ATN  
  (C) Fibrous scar—would follow chronic injury or infarction, not typical of fully recovered ATN  
  (D) Granulation tissue—part of wound healing in interstitial or infarcted tissue, not in normal tubular repair  
  (E) Normal kidney—correct, since the patient’s renal function and tubular architecture have returned to normal  

Final answer: \boxed{Normal\ kidney}

Step 1: Identify the type of bias prevented by requiring chest x-rays in both cases and controls.  
- If controls did not all have chest x-rays, some mesothelioma cases could go undiagnosed in the control group, inflating exposure prevalence among “controls.”  

Step 2: Match opportunity for disease detection.  
- By ensuring equal diagnostic scrutiny, you avoid differential misclassification of disease status.  

Step 3: Recognize that this design feature addresses ascertainment (detection) bias.  

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Minimize ascertainment bias (C)}}

Step 1: Recognize the presentation  
– A 68-year-old patient with 4 weeks of bilateral shoulder/upper arm stiffness, worse in the morning and improving later in the day  
– Associated low-grade fever and elevated ESR (88 mm/h)  
– Normal CK (rules out inflammatory myopathy), normal shoulder ROM, absence of tenderness  

Step 2: Establish the diagnosis  
– Age >50, proximal girdle stiffness, elevated inflammatory markers → polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR)  

Step 3: Identify risk factors for PMR  
– Required: age >50 (risk rises with advancing age)  
– Other associations: female sex (∼2:1 female : male), Northern European ancestry, and overlap with giant cell arteritis  
– Statin use can cause myalgias but not the classic picture of PMR  

Step 4: Match the choices  
(A) Age – strongest risk factor (virtually all patients are >50; risk increases with age)  
(B) Gender – female predominance, but less potent than age  
(C) Osteoarthritis – no established link with PMR  
(D) Previous occupation – no relevance  
(E) Pravastatin – may cause myalgias but not PMR  

Final answer: \boxed{A}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical syndrome  
– A syrinx (syringomyelia) in the cervical spinal cord classically presents with a “cape-like” loss of pain and temperature sensation in the upper extremities and upper trunk, along with motor weakness.

Step 2: Recall common causes of syringomyelia  
– Chiari I malformation (tonsillar herniation)  
– Spinal cord tumors (e.g., ependymoma)  
– Post-traumatic syrinx formation (can appear months to years after spinal cord injury)

Step 3: Apply to this patient  
– She has progressive arm weakness and sensory loss for 2 months, plus a week of headache and back pain.  
– An underlying neoplasm could cause weight loss but is less specific.  
– Chiari malformation usually presents earlier and is often diagnosed in adolescence.  
– Traumatic spinal cord injury can lead to a delayed post-traumatic syrinx.

Step 4: Determine the most pertinent history  
– Asking specifically about any prior spinal trauma (even if remote) is crucial because post-traumatic syrinxes often develop months after injury.

Therefore, the most appropriate additional history is a history of prior trauma.

Final Answer: \boxed{D}

Step 1: Identify the clinical syndrome  
The patient has signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism: fatigue, dry skin, brittle hair, weight gain, cold intolerance, bradycardia (55/min), delayed relaxation of Achilles reflex, and nonpitting edema (“myxedema”).

Step 2: Note the thyroid exam  
She has a nontender, symmetrically enlarged thyroid (goiter), without nodularity.

Step 3: Match to most likely cause  
– Hashimoto (chronic lymphocytic) thyroiditis is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in an iodine‐sufficient area. It produces a firm, nontender goiter and progressive thyroid failure over months to years.  
– Other choices are less consistent:  
  • Dyshormonogenesis (T4 biosynthesis defect) usually presents earlier in life (congenital goiter).  
  • Graves′ disease causes hyperthyroidism.  
  • Multinodular goiter is typically euthyroid or causes hyperthyroidism (“toxic” goiter).  
  • Riedel thyroiditis gives a hard, “woody” thyroid and often local compressive symptoms.  
  • Thyroid cysts and lymphoma are focal processes and would not cause the diffuse, chronic goiter with systemic hypothyroidism.

Therefore, the most likely diagnosis is chronic lymphocytic (Hashimoto) thyroiditis.

Final answer: \boxed{A}

Step 1: Recall the Starling forces in glomerular filtration:  
 • P_GC = hydrostatic pressure in glomerular capillary (favors filtration)  
 • P_BS = hydrostatic pressure in Bowman's space (opposes filtration)  
 • π_GC = oncotic pressure in glomerular capillary (opposes filtration)  
 • π_BS ≈ 0 (usually negligible)

Net filtration = (P_GC – P_BS) – (π_GC – π_BS)

Step 2: Understand the effect of efferent arteriole constriction.  
 • Constricting the efferent arteriole raises resistance downstream of the glomerular capillary bed.  
 • This causes blood to “back up” in the glomerular capillaries, increasing P_GC.  
 • An increased filtration fraction also concentrates plasma proteins in the capillary, gradually raising π_GC, but the primary immediate change is in P_GC.

Step 3: Match to answer choices.  
 (A) Kf – unchanged by vascular clamp  
 (B) Decreased hydrostatic pressure – incorrect (it increases)  
 (C) Decreased oncotic pressure – incorrect (oncotic rises)  
 (D) Increased hydrostatic pressure – correct (P_GC ↑)  
 (E) Increased oncotic pressure – this does occur secondarily, but the dominant immediate Starling force change is hydrostatic pressure

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Increased hydrostatic pressure (D)}}

Step 1: Identify the pattern of renal injury  
– BUN = 73, Cr = 4.2 → BUN∶Cr ≃ 17:1  
– Fractional excretions (not given) would likely be high after days of obstruction, but the key is imaging.  

Step 2: Use imaging/clinical findings to localize the lesion  
– Bladder ultrasound: marked bladder distention  
– Bilateral hydronephrosis  
→ This is postrenal (obstructive) acute kidney injury  

Step 3: Determine the cause of obstruction  
– Voiding attempts fail; patient has urinary retention with a distended bladder  
– Prostate is normal size, no evidence of prostatic enlargement/abscess  
– Prior catheterizations were successful, current urethral catheter attempts are unsuccessful  

Step 4: Choose the appropriate next step  
– In postrenal AKI, relief of obstruction is the first priority  
– Urethral catheterization has failed  
– Bethanechol (a cholinomimetic) would only help if there were detrusor underactivity, but here the bladder is mechanically obstructed and cannot be catheterized  
– Prazosin would treat prostatic obstruction but the prostate is normal size  
– Hemodialysis/ultrafiltration are supportive measures for severe uremia or refractory fluid overload but do not address the cause  
– Placement of a suprapubic catheter decompresses the bladder directly and relieves the obstruction  

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Placement of a suprapubic catheter}}

Step 1: Identify the murmur and pulse findings  
– A harsh systolic murmur heard best at the left sternal border in the 3rd–4th interspaces, plus weak femoral pulses with strong brachial pulses and a radiofemoral delay, is classic for coarctation of the aorta (post‐ductal).

Step 2: Understand the hemodynamics  
– In post‐ductal coarctation, the aortic narrowing is distal to the left subclavian artery.  
– Blood pressure is elevated proximal to the obstruction (upper extremities, head) and reduced distal to it (lower extremities).

Step 3: Anticipate long‐term complications  
– The sustained proximal hypertension predisposes to:  
  • Cerebral berry aneurysms → subarachnoid hemorrhage  
  • Early systemic hypertension with end‐organ damage (e.g., LV hypertrophy, risk of stroke)  
– Other listed choices are not typical complications:  
  • Atrial fibrillation – associated with atrial enlargement, not coarctation  
  • Cor pulmonale – from pulmonary hypertension, not seen here  
  • Tricuspid regurgitation – usually from right‐sided volume overload or pulmonary HTN, not coarctation

Final answer:  
\boxed{Systemic\ hypertension}

Step 1: Identify the patient’s cardiovascular risk factors and current measurements  
- Obese (BMI 38) with family history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, and coronary disease  
- Persistent blood pressure 156/98 mm Hg despite lifestyle modification → this is stage 2 hypertension  

Step 2: Apply guideline indications for pharmacotherapy  
- Hypertension: Stage 2 hypertension (≥140/90 mm Hg) warrants initiation of antihypertensive medication  
- Dyslipidemia: LDL 137 mg/dL in a 28-year-old without diabetes, clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, or an LDL ≥190 mg/dL does not yet meet guideline thresholds for statin therapy  
- Prediabetes (fasting glucose 104 mg/dL) is managed first with lifestyle changes, not pharmacotherapy  
- No indication for heart‐rate–lowering drugs (pulse 102/min is mildly elevated but not pathologically so)  
- No indication for appetite suppressants as first‐line therapy (lifestyle modification remains primary)  

Conclusion: The most urgent target for pharmacotherapy in this patient is his elevated blood pressure.  
Final Answer: \boxed{D}

Step 1: Key clinical features  
- Remote (5-year) history of a thigh gunshot wound  
- Now: “buzzing” sensation at the scar site  
- Loud murmur on auscultation with a palpable thrill  
- Dilated, varicose lower‐leg veins with incompetent valves  

Step 2: What do these findings indicate?  
- A bruit (“murmur”) + thrill = turbulent flow between high‐pressure and low‐pressure vessels  
- Varicose veins and valve incompetence imply chronically increased venous pressure and flow  

Step 3: Differential considerations  
(A) Arterial spasm – transient, no long‐term murmur/thrill or varicosities  
(C) Deep venous thrombosis – pain/swelling but no continuous bruit/thrill  
(D) Superficial femoral artery occlusion – would cause ischemia, not bruit/thrill  
(E) Pseudoaneurysm – can produce a bruit, but usually forms a pulsatile mass rather than causing widespread venous hypertension and varicosities  

Step 4: Best fit – Arteriovenous fistula  
- A missed AV fistula from the original injury can remain asymptomatic for years, then present with a continuous bruit, palpable thrill, and secondary venous dilatation/valve incompetence from shunted arterial blood into the venous system.  

Final answer: \boxed{Arteriovenous\;fistula\;(B)}

Step 1: Identify the stone type and metabolic abnormality  
– The patient passed a renal calculus shown to be composed primarily of calcium.  
– His 24-hour urine collection reveals increased calcium excretion (hypercalciuria), a risk factor for recurrent calcium stones.

Step 2: Recall diuretic effects on calcium handling  
– Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (e.g., acetazolamide) increase urine pH and promote calcium phosphate precipitation; they also cause increased urinary calcium excretion.  
– Loop diuretics (Na+–K+–2Cl− symporter inhibitors, e.g., furosemide) cause calciuresis (increased urinary calcium).  
– Osmotic diuretics (e.g., mannitol) likewise increase urinary calcium excretion passively.  
– ENaC inhibitors (e.g., amiloride) have minimal effect on calcium excretion.  
– Thiazide diuretics (Na+–Cl− symporter inhibitors, e.g., hydrochlorothiazide) increase calcium reabsorption in the distal tubule, thereby lowering urinary calcium excretion.

Step 3: Choose the agent that reduces urinary calcium  
– The most appropriate pharmacotherapy to reduce hypercalciuria and prevent recurrent calcium stones is a thiazide diuretic, which acts at the Na+–Cl− symporter.

Final Answer:
\boxed{\text{Na}^+\!-\!\text{Cl}^- \text{ symport inhibitor (thiazide diuretic)}}

Step 1: Identify key features  
- 8‐year‐old boy with 3 days of fever (up to 39.4 °C), sore throat, and itchy/red eyes  
- Physical exam: conjunctival injection + discharge (conjunctivitis) and oropharyngeal erythema (pharyngitis)  
- Epidemiology: outbreak among campers and volunteers; recent swimming in a camp pool  

Step 2: Narrow the differential  
- Pharyngoconjunctival fever (fever + pharyngitis + conjunctivitis) in an outbreak setting—classic for adenovirus, especially types 3 and 7, often linked to swimming pools.  
- CMV and EBV cause mononucleosis syndromes but do not typically cause conjunctivitis or outbreaks in camps.  
- Influenza can cause fever and sore throat but not usually conjunctivitis or point‐source outbreaks.  
- West Nile virus causes febrile illness with possible neuroinvasive disease, not pharyngitis or conjunctivitis.  

Step 3: Match the pathogen  
Adenovirus is known to cause outbreaks of pharyngoconjunctival fever, especially in settings with close contact and shared swimming pools.  

Final Answer  
\boxed{A\;\text{Adenovirus}}

Step 1: Clinical features  
– 45-year-old with 3 weeks of progressive epigastric burning pain that is worse at night and a few hours after eating  
– Weight loss, positive stool guaiac, 3 cm bleeding ulcer in the gastric antrum  

Step 2: Histology  
– Steiner silver stain (curved, comma-shaped rods) in the gastric mucosa adjacent to the ulcer  

Step 3: Organism and pathogenesis  
– These morphologic features are classic for Helicobacter pylori  
– H. pylori produces urease (to buffer gastric acid) and proteases/cytotoxins (VacA, CagA) that degrade mucus and injure the epithelium, leading to ulcer formation  

Step 4: Match to answer choices  
(A) “Elaboration of proteases and urease with local tissue destruction” correctly describes H. pylori pathogenesis  
(B) Hyperacidity alone would suggest a Zollinger–Ellison syndrome, not H. pylori  
(C) Preformed toxins in water (e.g., Vibrio cholerae) causes secretory diarrhea, not ulcers  
(D) Spirochetes invade gastric cells describes Treponema pallidum in syphilis, not a gastric ulcer  

Final answer:  
\boxed{A}

Step 1: Interpret the clinical picture  
– A 6-day-old breast-fed infant with poor feeding, vomiting, jaundice and hepatomegaly  
– Urine “reducing substances” positive, but glucose oxidase negative ⇒ a non-glucose reducing sugar (e.g. galactose or fructose) is spilling into the urine  

Step 2: Narrow the differential  
– Fructosemia (e.g. essential fructosuria or hereditary fructose intolerance) usually presents when infant begins fruit juices or formula with sucrose/fructose; less likely in an exclusively breast-fed 6-day-old  
– Galactosemia (classical) presents soon after breast milk or formula feeding (both contain lactose → galactose + glucose)  

Step 3: Recall the enzyme defect and its metabolic consequence  
– Classical galactosemia is caused by galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase deficiency  
– This leads to accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate (and some galactose) in liver, kidney, brain  

Step 4: Match with answer choices  
(A) Fructose 1,6-bisphosphate – accumulates in fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency (not this case)  
(B) Galactose 1-phosphate – correct for classic galactosemia  
(C) Glucose 1-phosphate – accumulates in some glycogen storage diseases (e.g. debranching enzyme deficiency)  
(D) Glucose 6-phosphate – accumulates in von Gierke disease (G6Pase deficiency)  

Final Answer: \boxed{B} Galactose 1-phosphate

Step 1: Identify that this is new‐onset hypertension in a middle‐aged woman.  
Step 2: Note the laboratory finding of hypokalemia (K+ 3.1 mEq/L) in the setting of hypertension.  
Step 3: Recall that primary hyperaldosteronism (“Conn syndrome”) causes excess aldosterone secretion, leading to Na+ retention (↑BP) and K+ wasting (hypokalemia).  
Step 4: Exclude the other choices:
• Cushing syndrome – would have characteristic fat redistribution, muscle weakness, hyperglycemia  
• Fibromuscular dysplasia – typically in younger women; does not usually cause hypokalemia  
• Hypothyroidism – does not cause hypokalemia or significant hypertension  
• Obstructive sleep apnea – may cause hypertension but not hypokalemia  

Final answer: \boxed{Hyperaldosteronism\ (\text{Choice C})}

Step 1: Identify the problem  
– This patient with C. difficile–like diarrhea has signs of volume depletion (dry mucous membranes, cracked lips), a BUN/Cr ratio ≈30/1.5=20 (prerenal azotemia), and hypernatremia (Na⁺ 150 mEq/L) from free‐water losses.

Step 2: Determine goals of fluid therapy  
– Restore intravascular volume, correct prerenal azotemia, and begin gradual correction of hypernatremia.  
– Isotonic crystalloid is preferred initially to expand extracellular volume.

Step 3: Evaluate fluid choices  
– Bicarbonate: not indicated (HCO₃⁻ 22 mEq/L is essentially normal).  
– Calcium gluconate: used for severe hyperkalemia, not needed here (K⁺ 3.7 mEq/L).  
– 5% dextrose in water (D5W): hypotonic; would worsen intravascular volume deficit if given first.  
– 0.9% saline: isotonic; restores ECF volume and improves perfusion, also begins to correct Na⁺.

Final answer:  
\boxed{0.9\%\ \text{Saline}}

Step 1: Identify the presentation  
A young, healthy marathon runner collapses at the finish line, feels dizzy and light‐headed (but remains alert), and is found to be tachycardic (120/min) and hypotensive (85/50 mm Hg).

Step 2: Consider the most likely cause  
– Collapse immediately after prolonged exercise suggests volume depletion (sweating, insufficient fluid/electrolyte replacement).  
– No focal neurologic signs, no chest pain, no nausea/vomiting, no medication use.

Step 3: Review the response to treatment  
– Feet elevated + IV 0.9% saline rapidly restore her pulse to 70/min and BP to 110/70 mm Hg, and her symptoms resolve.

Step 4: Eliminate other choices  
(A) Autonomic dysfunction – would not correct so quickly just with fluids.  
(B) Hypokalemia – presents with muscle weakness/cramps, not acute orthostatic symptoms, and would not be rapidly corrected by saline alone.  
(C) Hyponatremia – typically causes headache, nausea, confusion, seizures, not isolated dizziness that resolves with saline.  
(D) Myocardial inflammation – myocarditis presents with chest pain, arrhythmias, heart‐failure signs; not seen here.

Step 5: Recognize orthostatic (postural) hypotension from acute volume depletion  
– Orthostatic hypotension is defined by an excessive drop in BP upon standing due to decreased intravascular volume; the rapid response to fluid and leg elevation confirms volume‐depletion–mediated orthostatic hypotension.

Final Answer: \boxed{E} orthostatic hypotension

Step 1: Recognize the clinical setting  
– A term parturient in active labor suddenly develops acute dyspnea, cyanosis, unresponsiveness, and cardiovascular collapse.  

Step 2: Note timing and precipitating event  
– The event occurs during the second stage of labor, immediately following a prolonged fetal deceleration.  

Step 3: Identify associated laboratory/clinical findings  
– Five minutes into resuscitation, diffuse bleeding from the nose, mouth, and IV sites appears, consistent with disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC).  

Step 4: Differentiate the diagnoses  
– Intracerebral hemorrhage would present with focal neurologic signs rather than acute pulmonary distress and DIC.  
– Myocardial infarction in a young woman is extremely unlikely, and would not typically cause acute DIC.  
– Status asthmaticus causes severe bronchospasm but not sudden cardiovascular collapse or DIC.  
– Toxic shock syndrome is a subacute febrile illness, not an immediate peripartum catastrophe with DIC.  
– Amniotic fluid embolism classically presents with sudden respiratory distress, cardiovascular collapse, and coagulopathy (DIC) during labor or immediately postpartum.  

Final diagnosis:  

\boxed{Amniotic\ fluid\ embolism}

Step 1: Recall definitions  
- Potency = how far left the dose–response curve is (i.e. lower EC₅₀).  
- Efficacy = the maximum effect (Emax) the drug can produce.  

Step 2: Read the curves  
- Drug Y’s curve is shifted furthest to the left of X and Z, so Y has the lowest EC₅₀ → highest potency.  
- Drugs X and Y reach the same maximal relaxation, but Z plateaus lower (a partial agonist) → Z has lower efficacy.  

Step 3: Match to answer choices  
The best statement is that Drug Y is the most potent of the three.  

Final Answer: \boxed{C}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical picture  
– A 3-month-old with sudden cyanosis, low pulse oximetry (82%) unresponsive to supplemental O₂, and “chocolate-brown” blood suggests methemoglobinemia rather than true hypoxemia.  

Step 2: Understand methemoglobinemia  
– In methemoglobin, the iron in heme is oxidized from Fe²⁺ (ferrous) to Fe³⁺ (ferric), which cannot bind O₂.  
– Methemoglobin also shifts the remaining Fe²⁺–heme sites toward tighter O₂ binding (left shift), further impairing O₂ delivery.  

Step 3: Identify the most common enzymatic defect in congenital methemoglobinemia  
– Normally, NADH-dependent methemoglobin reductase (cytochrome b₅ reductase) reduces Fe³⁺ back to Fe²⁺.  
– A congenital deficiency of this enzyme leads to ↑ methemoglobin, cyanosis, and chocolate-colored blood.  

Step 4: Match the defect to the answer choices  
(A) Decreased activity in the enzyme that reduces Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺ → ↓methemoglobin reductase activity → methemoglobinemia.  
(B) Decreased erythrocyte production → anemia, not chocolate blood or cyanosis unresponsive to O₂.  
(C) Inability of the porphyrin ring to stay attached → would cause hemolysis, not classic chocolate-brown blood.  
(D) Increased iron in the body → hemochromatosis or hemosiderosis, not acute cyanosis.  
(E) Mutation of one of the codons for hemoglobin → more consistent with a structural hemoglobinopathy (e.g., sickle cell), not sudden methemoglobinemia.  

Final Answer  
The most likely cause is a deficiency of the enzyme that reduces Fe³⁺ (methemoglobin) back to Fe²⁺.  

\boxed{A}

Step 1: Recognize DKA and initial treatment  
– This 12-year-old with type 1 DM has classic diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA): hyperglycemia (glucose 563 mg/dL), anion-gap metabolic acidosis (pH 7.20, HCO3– 10, PCO2 22), dehydration, and compensated respiratory alkalosis.  
– Initial management is isotonic fluid resuscitation (0.9% saline) and an insulin infusion.  

Step 2: Identify new neurologic signs after 3 h of therapy  
– The patient becomes somnolent, develops headache, has an emetic episode, urinary incontinence, and low urine output.  
– These are concerning for cerebral edema, a known complication of DKA treatment in children (usually 4–12 h after initiation of therapy).  

Step 3: Exclude other answer choices  
(A) Bicarbonate: Generally not given in pediatric DKA except for extreme acidosis (pH <6.9). It also increases cerebral edema risk.  
(B) Dexamethasone: Used for raised intracranial pressure from tumors or vasogenic edema, not osmotic cytotoxic edema in DKA.  
(C) 50% Dextrose: Would worsen hyperosmolality shifts; only given if hypoglycemia develops.  
(D) Furosemide: Diuresis worsens volume depletion and hypotension; not indicated for cerebral edema in DKA.  

Step 4: Treat cerebral edema with an osmotic agent  
– The treatment of choice for suspected cerebral edema in DKA is an osmotic diuretic, classically intravenous mannitol.  

Final Answer: \boxed{Mannitol\,} (Choice E)

Step‐by‐step reasoning:

1. Presentation  
   – A 35-year-old man with pneumonia unresponsive to ceftriaxone plus azithromycin  
   – Hilar adenopathy on chest X-ray, blood‐streaked sputum  
   – Returned 1 week ago from the southwestern United States  

2. Key laboratory findings  
   – PPD negative (makes TB less likely)  
   – AFB smears negative  
   – Peripheral eosinophilia (15% of WBCs)  
   – Blood cultures negative  

3. Epidemiology and clinical clues  
   – Southwestern U.S. travel/occupational exposure → risk for Coccidioides immitis (“Valley fever”)  
   – Eosinophilia is a classical clue in coccidioidomycosis  
   – Presentation 1–3 weeks after exposure fits the incubation period  

4. Diagnostic options  
   A. Bacterial sputum culture – already covered by empirical antibiotics and unhelpful after 3 days of therapy  
   B. Chest CT – may show nodules or cavities but is not diagnostic for a specific etiology  
   C. Fungal blood culture – Coccidioides is usually diagnosed by serology, and blood cultures are low yield (plus risk of laboratory exposure)  
   D. Serologic studies for Coccidioides immitis – the standard, most sensitive and specific test for coccidioidomycosis  
   E. Sputum cytology – may rarely show spherules but is much less sensitive than serology  
   F. Urine Legionella antigen – useful for Legionella pneumophila, but that infection usually presents with hyponatremia, GI symptoms, and lacks eosinophilia and hilar adenopathy  

5. Conclusion  
   The most likely diagnosis is acute coccidioidomycosis. The best initial diagnostic test is serologic testing for Coccidioides immitis.

Final Answer: \boxed{D}

Step 1: Identify the clinical scenario  
– A 62-year-old man with urinary hesitancy, dribbling, and nocturia  
– Physical exam: diffusely enlarged, firm, nontender prostate  
→ This is most consistent with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH).

Step 2: Recall the pathophysiology of BPH  
– BPH is driven by dihydrotestosterone (DHT), which acts on androgen receptors in prostatic stromal and epithelial cells to stimulate growth.  
– DHT is formed from testosterone by the enzyme 5α-reductase.

Step 3: Match the answer choices to the pathophysiology  
(A) Activation of the α1-adrenergic receptor → contributes to increased smooth muscle tone in the prostate/bladder neck (symptom mediator), not growth  
(B) Conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone → key step in the development of BPH  
(C) Conversion of testosterone to estradiol → implicated in bone and some sexual functions, not the main driver of BPH  
(D) Inhibition of the α1-adrenergic receptor → therapeutic approach (e.g., tamsulosin), not a cause  
(E) Production of prostate-specific antigen → a marker released by prostate cells, not a cause of hyperplasia  

Step 4: Select the best answer  
The critical step in the development of BPH is the conversion of testosterone to the more active androgen DHT by 5α-reductase.

Final Answer:  
\boxed{Conversion\ of\ testosterone\ to\ dihydrotestosterone}

Step 1: Identify the key finding  
The patient’s serum glucose is elevated at 220 mg/dL in the setting of critical illness one day after severe trauma.

Step 2: Consider possible causes of hyperglycemia in this context  
- Exogenous glucose infusion (choice D) may contribute but standard 5% dextrose in LR provides only modest glucose load.  
- Renal impairment (choice C) is unlikely: creatinine is normal at 1 mg/dL.  
- Bacterial production of glucose (choice B) does not occur in vivo.  
- Absolute insulin deficiency (choice E) would be unlikely in an acute trauma patient without diabetes history.  
- Stress‐mediated insulin resistance (choice A) occurs commonly in critically ill or injured patients due to high levels of cortisol, catecholamines, and inflammatory cytokines, leading to decreased glucose uptake by muscle and fat.

Step 3: Mechanism of stress‐induced hyperglycemia  
In severe trauma or critical illness, counterregulatory hormones (epinephrine, cortisol, growth hormone) and cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1) rise sharply. These factors:  
• Increase hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis  
• Impair insulin signaling in peripheral tissues (muscle and adipose), reducing GLUT4‐mediated glucose uptake  

Result: Blood glucose rises despite normal or even elevated insulin levels (relative insulin resistance).

Final Answer: \boxed{Decreased\ uptake\ of\ glucose\ by\ insulin-sensitive\ tissues\ (A)}

Step 1: Recognize the murmur and clinical context  
– The patient has a diastolic rumble best heard at the apex, a history of “always sick as a child” (rheumatic fever risk), exertional dyspnea, and new-onset atrial fibrillation (irregular pulse, palpitations).  
– This picture is most consistent with rheumatic mitral stenosis leading to left atrial enlargement and atrial fibrillation.

Step 2: Identify what diagnostic study is most helpful  
– The definitive evaluation of valvular anatomy and gradient is by echocardiography (not listed).  
– Of the options given, a chest x-ray is the next best study: it can demonstrate left atrial enlargement (“double density” sign, straightening of the left heart border) and pulmonary venous congestion, supporting the diagnosis of mitral stenosis.  
– An ECG would only confirm atrial fibrillation (already apparent clinically), and the laboratory tests (ASO titer, CRP, TSH) would not address valve structure or hemodynamics at this stage.

Final Answer: \boxed{A}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical presentation  
– A premature (30-week) neonate, now 2 weeks old, with  
  • Feeding intolerance (increased gastric residuals)  
  • Abdominal distention and tenderness  
  • Bloody stools  
  • Systemic signs (temperature instability, hypotension, thrombocytopenia)  
These findings are classic for necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC).

Step 2: Identify the diagnostic test for NEC  
– The most specific and readily available test is an abdominal radiograph, which can demonstrate:  
  • Pneumatosis intestinalis (air within the bowel wall)  
  • Portal venous gas  
  • Free intraperitoneal air if perforation has occurred  

Step 3: Match to the answer choices  
(A) Coagulation studies → assess for DIC but not diagnostic for NEC  
(B) Stool reducing substances → evaluate carbohydrate malabsorption  
(C) Stool culture → evaluate for infectious diarrhea  
(D) Upper GI series → evaluate malrotation/volvulus, not first‐line for NEC  
(E) Abdominal X-ray → the correct test to confirm NEC  

Final Answer: \boxed{E}

Step 1: Identify the MRI findings.  
On the lumbar‐spine MRI the intervertebral discs appear dark on T2‐weighted images (loss of the bright, hydrated nucleus pulposus signal) and show decreased disc height with marginal osteophyte formation. These are characteristic signs of degenerative disc disease (spondylosis).

Step 2: Recall risk factors for intervertebral disc degeneration.  
Key contributors include:  
• Age (natural “wear and tear”)  
• Obesity (increased mechanical load)  
• Cigarette smoking (impairs disc nutrition by reducing microvascular blood flow)  

Step 3: Examine the patient’s history for these risk factors.  
• Age 57 → moderate risk due to aging  
• BMI 33 → obesity, but he attributes weight gain mainly to inactivity  
• Smoking history: 2 packs/day for 25 years → a major risk factor  
• Alcohol use, diclofenac, loratadine, remote opioid use → none are known to directly cause disc degeneration  

Step 4: Select the factor that “most strongly contributed” to the MRI changes.  
Although aging and obesity play roles, the strongest modifiable aggravator in this patient is his long, heavy smoking history, which directly impairs nutrient diffusion into the avascular disc and accelerates degeneration.

Final answer: \boxed{\text{Cigarette smoking}} (Choice B)

Step 1: Identify the bony injury on the X-ray  
- The radiograph shows a clear fracture through the proximal ulna shaft.  

Step 2: Look for joint dislocation  
- The radial head is no longer articulating with the capitellum (it is dislocated).  

Step 3: Match the pattern to named fracture‐dislocations  
- A fracture of the proximal ulna with dislocation of the radial head is a Monteggia lesion.  

Step 4: Choose the answer that describes “fractured ulna, dislocated radius”  
- That corresponds to choice (D).  

Final Answer: \boxed{fractured\ ulna,\ dislocated\ radius}

Step 1: Identify the drugs and their key actions  
- Atorvastatin: HMG‐CoA reductase inhibitor – lowers LDL and triglycerides, raises HDL  
- Losartan: angiotensin II type 1 (AT₁) receptor blocker – prevents ang II from exerting its effects at AT₁  

Step 2: Recall the renin–angiotensin negative‐feedback loop  
- Normally, angiotensin II binding to AT₁ on juxtaglomerular cells inhibits renin release.  
- If you block AT₁ (with losartan), ang II can no longer suppress renin.  

Step 3: Predict what happens when AT₁ is blocked  
- Plasma renin activity ↑ (loss of negative feedback)  
- Ang II levels may actually rise, but it cannot act at AT₁ to stimulate aldosterone secretion  
- Aldosterone levels ↓  

Step 4: Match to the answer choices  
We expect “increased” renin and “decreased” aldosterone, which is choice C.  

Final Answer: \boxed{C}

Step 1: Identify the methotrexate target  
- Methotrexate is a folic acid analog that inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR).  
- DHFR converts dihydrofolate (DHF) → tetrahydrofolate (THF).  
- THF (and its derivatives) are essential one-carbon carriers needed for synthesis of thymidine and purines.

Step 2: Understand leucovorin (“folinic acid”) rescue  
- Leucovorin is 5-formyl-THF, a reduced form of folate that does not require DHFR for activation.  
- When given along with high-dose methotrexate, leucovorin supplies downstream folate cofactors to normal cells, rescuing them from DHFR blockade.

Step 3: Determine which enzyme’s requirement is bypassed  
- Since leucovorin already is a form of THF, it circumvents the need for DHFR.  

Final answer: the rescue agent bypasses the requirement for dihydrofolate reductase.  

\boxed{A}

Step 1: List key features of the patient’s episodes  
– Acute onset rotatory vertigo and imbalance  
– Associated decreased hearing, tinnitus, a feeling of fullness in the right ear  
– Episodes last about 12 hours (two such episodes over 3 months)  
– Exam between episodes shows mild sensorineural hearing loss on the right  

Step 2: Compare with each diagnostic option  
(A) Acoustic neuroma  
  – Gradual, progressive unilateral hearing loss and tinnitus; vertigo is rare and not episodic  
  – Doesn’t present as recurrent 12-hour attacks  

(B) Benign positional vertigo (BPPV)  
  – Brief (seconds to a minute) vertigo triggered by head position changes  
  – No hearing loss, tinnitus, or ear fullness  

(C) Brain stem transient ischemic attacks  
  – Vertigo/imbalance might be transient (minutes) and often accompanied by other brainstem signs (diplopia, dysphagia)  
  – No ear fullness or tinnitus  

(D) Meniere’s disease  
  – Episodic vertigo lasting 20 minutes to several hours (often 2–24 hours)  
  – Sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, ear fullness (“hydrops”)  
  – Relapsing–remitting course  

(E) Viral labyrinthitis  
  – Continuous vertigo lasting days to weeks after a viral prodrome  
  – Usually no fluctuating hearing loss or tinnitus once vertigo has started  

Step 3: Identify the best match  
The combination of recurrent 12-hour vertigo spells, unilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and ear fullness is classic for Meniere’s disease.

Final Answer: \boxed{Meniere’s\ disease\ (D)}.

Step 1: Summarize the key findings  
– 43-year-old woman with exertional “crushing” chest pain, ST depressions in V3–V6, negative troponins, and normal coronaries on cath  
– Since discharge she has had brief (2–4 min) recurrent chest pains of similar quality  
– No obstructive CAD—this is most consistent with “cardiac syndrome X” (microvascular angina)  

Step 2: Recall management of microvascular (syndrome X) angina  
– Patients have anginal‐type chest pain, positive stress tests, but normal coronary arteries  
– First‐line prophylactic therapy is with agents that improve microvascular perfusion:  
   • Calcium-channel blockers (eg, diltiazem, amlodipine)  
   • Nitrates  
– Beta-blockers can help in typical stable angina due to obstructive CAD, but CCBs are preferred here  

Step 3: Match to the answer choices  
(A) Diltiazem – a non-dihydropyridine CCB, effective for microvascular/vasospastic angina  
(B) Lisinopril – ACE inhibitors are used in hypertension/ventricular remodeling but not first-line for syndrome X  
(C) Propranolol – a beta-blocker more suited for obstructive CAD; less effective for microvascular angina  
(D) Warfarin – anticoagulation not indicated  
(E) No pharmacotherapy – inappropriate given recurrent anginal episodes  

Final Answer  
\boxed{Diltiazem}

Step 1: Characterize the presentation  
– Symptoms in both children and teachers: nasal congestion, sneezing, nonproductive cough, eye irritation  
– No fevers, systemic signs, or similar community outbreaks  

Step 2: Consider infectious versus environmental/irritant causes  
– Infectious causes (Mycoplasma, RSV, Legionella) typically produce fevers or systemic symptoms and are accompanied by broader community spread.  
– Environmental/irritant causes (e.g., mold, asbestos) can produce predominantly mucosal irritation without fever.  

Step 3: Link to the school’s recent history  
– Fire followed by water damage to classrooms and ventilation system → ideal conditions for mold growth.  
– Rapid repairs likely insufficient to eliminate all damp areas, allowing mold proliferation.  

Step 4: Identify the most likely culprit  
– Mold spores are known to cause allergic/irritant respiratory symptoms (rhinitis, nonproductive cough, conjunctival irritation) in exposed populations without fever.  

Final Answer  
\boxed{Mold\;spores\;(C)}

Step 1: Identify the problem  
– A patient with a massive liver laceration is actively bleeding.  

Step 2: Recall the Pringle maneuver  
– To control hepatic bleeding, surgeons can compress the hepatoduodenal ligament, which contains the portal triad (hepatic artery proper, portal vein, and common bile duct).  
– Compressing this ligament at the porta hepatis occludes inflow through the hepatic artery and portal vein, decreasing bleeding from the liver.

Step 3: Differentiate the ligaments  
– Coronary and triangular ligaments: peritoneal reflections that attach the liver to the diaphragm; do not contain the portal triad.  
– Falciform ligament: attaches the liver to the anterior abdominal wall; contains the ligamentum teres hepatis.  
– Gastrohepatic ligament: part of the lesser omentum between the liver and the lesser curvature of the stomach; contains small gastric vessels but not the main hepatic inflow.  
– Hepatoduodenal ligament: the free margin of the lesser omentum between the liver and duodenum; contains the portal triad.

Step 4: Answer  
Compressing the hepatoduodenal ligament (Pringle maneuver) will most effectively reduce bleeding from the liver.

Final answer:  
\boxed{D}

Step 1: Clinical presentation  
– A 30-year-old man with 6 weeks of colicky abdominal pain, diarrhea, and occasional blood.  
– Sudden peritonitis from intestinal perforation, fibrinous exudates, thickened adherent bowel loops with areas of narrowing (strictures).  

Step 2: Gross findings  
– “Skip” lesions: several discrete thickened segments in both small and large bowel.  
– Strictures leading to narrowing and ultimately perforation.  

Step 3: Histologic clues (photomicrograph)  
– Transmural inflammation (involving the full thickness of the bowel wall)  
– Noncaseating granulomas  

Step 4: Differential diagnosis  
– Ulcerative colitis: continuous mucosal disease of colon only, no transmural granulomas, pseudopolyps rather than strictures.  
– Ischemic necrosis: typically patchy mucosal infarction without well-formed granulomas or skip-lesion chronic changes.  
– Diverticulitis: outpouchings of mucosa, usually left colon in older patients, not granulomatous.  
– Colon cancer: mass lesion, dysplastic glands, not granulomas.  

Step 5: Correlation  
Transmural, granulomatous inflammation with skip lesions, strictures, and fistula/perforation is characteristic of Crohn disease.  

Final answer: \boxed{\text{Crohn disease}}

Step 1: Recognize the language barrier  
The patient speaks only Mandarin, and clear communication is essential for obtaining informed consent.

Step 2: Identify proper interpreter use  
– Family members (even spouses) should not be used for interpreting high‐stakes discussions (like diagnosis and consent) because of potential inaccuracies, omissions, or conflicts of interest.  
– Delaying consent until tomorrow’s in‐person interpreter unnecessarily prolongs patient anxiety and delays care.

Step 3: Choose a professional service available immediately  
Most hospitals have 24/7 telephone interpreter services, which satisfy the requirement for a qualified, neutral interpreter.

Final Answer: D (Use a telephone interpreter service)  

\boxed{D}

Step 1: Identify the hypersensitivity type  
– The rash after topical hair dye in a previously sensitized patient is classic for allergic contact dermatitis, a type IV (delayed) hypersensitivity reaction mediated by T cells.

Step 2: Outline the cellular mechanism  
– Haptens in the hair dye bind skin proteins and are taken up by epidermal Langerhans cells (a type of dendritic cell).  
– Langerhans cells process and present haptenated peptides on MHC II.  
– Naïve CD4+ T cells recognize the MHC II–peptide complex via their TCR (signal 1) and require a second (costimulatory) signal to become activated, proliferate, and differentiate into effector T cells.

Step 3: Recall the key costimulatory interaction for T‐cell activation  
– The essential costimulatory ligand–receptor pair is B7 (CD80/CD86) on the antigen‐presenting cell binding to CD28 on the T lymphocyte.

Step 4: Match to answer choices  
– Choice B: “CD28 on T lymphocytes → CD80 on epidermal Langerhans cells” correctly describes the critical costimulatory interaction.

Final answer: \boxed{B}

Step 1: Identify the patient’s affect and communication style  
- He’s giving one‐word, sarcastic answers, avoiding eye contact, and clearly frustrated by repeated questions.  

Step 2: Recognize the need for an empathic response  
- Before gathering more history about his pain, it’s critical to acknowledge his feelings and build rapport.  

Step 3: Evaluate the options  
(A) jumps back into biomedical questioning without addressing his frustration.  
(B) politely but bluntly insists on history‐taking, which may feel dismissive of his emotions.  
(C) postpones engagement and risks alienating him further.  
(D) is threatening and undermines the therapeutic relationship.  
(E) uses reflection (“You sound upset”) and invites him to elaborate on his feelings, which is the most empathic and rapport‐building approach.  

Final Answer: \boxed{E}

Step 1: Identify the communication breakdown  
- A pharmacy student observes that the patient missed two antibiotic doses but feels unable to speak up.  
- Neither the physician nor the nurse knew about the missed doses.  
- This reflects a hierarchical culture and lack of psychological safety: lower‐level team members don’t feel comfortable raising concerns.  

Step 2: Evaluate the answer choices  
(A) Conduct interdisciplinary rounds in a quieter location  
 – May reduce ambient noise, but doesn’t address the student’s reluctance to speak up due to hierarchy.  
(B) Encourage questions from all team members  
 – Directly promotes psychological safety and flattens hierarchy; team members feel empowered to voice concerns.  
(C) Implement a checklist for standardizing patient rounds  
 – Checklists improve consistency but won’t by themselves change team culture or empower quieter members.  
(D) Use computers during rounds to review medications  
 – May improve accuracy or access to data but can further discourage open discussion and nonverbal communication.

Step 3: Choose the best intervention  
– The core issue is lack of psychological safety; fostering an environment where every team member is invited and encouraged to ask questions and report errors will most likely prevent crucial information from being withheld.

Final Answer: \boxed{B}

Step 1: Identify the clinical syndrome  
– 2-week history of fever, sore throat with exudative tonsillitis, and generalized lymphadenopathy ⇒ “mononucleosis‐like” syndrome  

Step 2: Note the laboratory findings  
– Pancytopenia (Hb 9.6, WBC 1,500, plt 60,000)  
– Heterophile antibody test negative  

Step 3: Construct the differential for a mononucleosis‐like illness  
– Epstein–Barr virus (EBV) mononucleosis ⇒ usually heterophile positive and causes lymphocytosis rather than pancytopenia  
– Cytomegalovirus (CMV) mononucleosis ⇒ heterophile negative, but exudative pharyngitis is uncommon  
– Acute HIV infection ⇒ mononucleosis‐like syndrome with fever, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, cytopenias, heterophile negative  
– Streptococcal or gonococcal pharyngitis ⇒ do not cause pancytopenia or generalized lymphadenopathy  
– Lymphogranuloma venereum ⇒ presents with inguinal buboes, not exudative pharyngitis  

Step 4: Choose the best fit  
– Acute HIV infection produces a mononucleosis‐like syndrome, is heterophile negative, and commonly causes cytopenias.  

Final Answer: \boxed{HIV\ infection\ (acute\ retroviral\ syndrome)}

Step 1: Definitions  
- α (alpha) = 0.05 is the probability of a type I error (false positive).  
- β (beta) = 0.20 is the probability of a type II error (false negative).  
- Power = 1 – β = 0.80 is the probability of detecting a true effect.

Step 2: Effect of decreasing sample size  
- A smaller sample size reduces the study’s power (makes it harder to detect a true difference).  
- Lower power means β (the chance of a type II error) increases.  
- α remains fixed at 0.05 (assuming the same significance threshold).

Final answer:  
\boxed{Chance\;of\;a\;type\;II\;error\; (B)}

Step 1: Identify the diagnosis  
– A 25-year-old woman with severe crampy lower abdominal pain beginning on the first day of menses that lasts the entire menstrual period is most consistent with primary dysmenorrhea.  

Step 2: Understand the pathophysiology  
– Primary dysmenorrhea is caused by excessive release of prostaglandins (particularly PGF₂α) from the endometrium during menstruation.  
– These prostaglandins induce uterine hypercontractility, leading to ischemia and pain.  

Step 3: Choose first-line treatment  
– Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are the treatment of choice for primary dysmenorrhea.  
– NSAIDs work by inhibiting cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes, thereby reducing the synthesis of prostaglandins.  

Step 4: Match to the answer choices  
(A) Inhibition of estrogen synthesis – used in some hormonal therapies, not first-line for dysmenorrhea  
(B) Inhibition of 11β-hydroxylase activity – affects cortisol synthesis, not relevant  
(C) Inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis – mechanism of NSAIDs, correct for primary dysmenorrhea  
(D) Stimulation of follicle-stimulating hormone synthesis – would affect follicular growth, not pain relief  
(E) Stimulation of luteinizing hormone synthesis – would trigger ovulation, not pain relief  

Final answer: \boxed{Inhibition\ of\ prostaglandin\ synthesis}

Step 1: Identify the likely pathogen  
– Cat bites commonly inoculate Pasteurella multocida (a gram‐negative coccobacillus) along with oral anaerobes.

Step 2: Recognize the need for empiric coverage  
– Therapy must cover Pasteurella multocida and anaerobes.  
– Pasteurella is uniformly sensitive to penicillin; however, pure penicillin has no anaerobic β‐lactamase coverage.

Step 3: Select the appropriate antibiotic  
– Ampicillin–sulbactam provides reliable coverage for Pasteurella and for β‐lactamase–producing anaerobes.  
– Other options (e.g., dicloxacillin/clindamycin, cefazolin/gentamicin, levofloxacin, vancomycin/metronidazole) either lack Pasteurella activity or lack broad anaerobic coverage.

Final Answer: \boxed{Ampicillin\text{–}sulbactam}

Step 1: Recognize the clinical syndrome  
 – A young woman with secondary amenorrhea and galactorrhea → suggests hyperprolactinemia.  
 – Bitemporal hemianopia → mass effect on the optic chiasm (sellar mass).  

Step 2: Correlate laboratory and imaging findings  
 – Prolactin level is 100 ng/mL (elevated; normal <20 ng/mL).  
 – MRI shows a 1.2 × 1.5 cm pituitary mass compressing the optic chiasm → macroprolactinoma.  

Step 3: Determine the first‐line therapy for a macroprolactinoma  
 – Dopamine agonists (cabergoline or bromocriptine) reduce prolactin secretion, shrink the tumor, restore menses, and relieve optic compression.  
 – Cabergoline is preferred because of better efficacy and tolerability.  

Step 4: Exclude other options  
 (A) Biopsy – not needed; imaging and labs are diagnostic.  
 (C) Gamma knife radiation – reserved for refractory cases.  
 (D) OCPs – do not shrink the tumor or relieve mass effect.  
 (E) Transsphenoidal surgery – indicated only if dopamine agonist therapy fails or is not tolerated, or if there is apoplexy.  

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Cabergoline therapy}}

Step 1: Identify key features  
– 3-year-old boy with acute (2 day) onset of bulging (exophthalmos) of one eye  
– MRI: 2 cm mass involving the ocular muscles  
– Biopsy: malignant cells, some showing striations  

Step 2: Interpret “striations” in a tumor  
Striations in tumor cells imply differentiation toward skeletal muscle (cross-striations on histology are characteristic of skeletal muscle fibers).  

Step 3: Consider pediatric tumors with striated muscle differentiation  
The malignant tumor derived from skeletal muscle precursors is rhabdomyosarcoma. It is the most common soft-tissue sarcoma in children and often presents in the orbit, causing proptosis.  

Step 4: Exclude other choices  
(A) Neuroblastoma → neural crest tumor, “small blue cells,” Homer-Wright rosettes, often abdominal; orbital metastases can cause “raccoon eyes” but no striations.  
(B) Pheochromocytoma → catecholamine-secreting adrenal medulla tumor; presents with hypertension, not orbital mass.  
(C) Retinoblastoma → intraocular tumor of retinal cells in infants; presents with leukocoria, not an orbital muscle mass.  
(E) Thyroid cancer → can invade locally or metastasize, but would not show skeletal muscle striations.  

Final Answer  
\boxed{Rhabdomyosarcoma\;(D)}

Step 1: Clinical presentation  
– A 75-year-old with a 2-year history of progressive dyspnea, dry crackles at both lung bases, digital clubbing, and reticular opacities on chest X-ray suggests an interstitial lung process (most likely idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis).  

Step 2: Laboratory and imaging clues  
– Normal CBC/chemistries/urinalysis (no evidence of connective-tissue disease or renal involvement)  
– No signs of left- or right-sided heart failure (normal JVP, no edema)  
– No hilar lymphadenopathy (less likely sarcoidosis)  

Step 3: Next best diagnostic step  
– The next step in evaluating suspected interstitial lung disease is high-resolution CT (HRCT) of the chest, which can noninvasively characterize the pattern (e.g., usual interstitial pneumonia) and often obviate the need for surgical lung biopsy.  

Step 4: Rationale against other options  
– Echocardiography (A) evaluates cardiac causes of dyspnea, but clinical/radiographic features point to a parenchymal lung disease.  
– Lung biopsy (C) is invasive and reserved for cases where HRCT is nondiagnostic.  
– Anti-GBM assay (D) is for Goodpasture’s syndrome, which presents with hemoptysis/renal disease, not a chronic dry cough and reticular pattern.  
– PET scan (E) is used for malignancy staging, not for diffuse interstitial lung disease.  

Final Answer  
\boxed{\text{High‐resolution CT scan of the chest (B)}}

Step 1: Characterize the back pain  
– Chronicity: 1 year of recurrent episodes  
– Pattern: Resolves in ≈2 weeks with rest/analgesics, recurs every 2–3 months  
– Examination: Normal, no neurologic deficits, no “red‐flag” signs (fever, weight loss, cauda equina, night pain, trauma)

Step 2: Identify red‐flag criteria for imaging or invasive interventions  
– None present (no neurologic deficits, systemic symptoms, or history of malignancy/trauma)

Step 3: Recognize risk factors amenable to modification  
– BMI of 37 kg/m2 (obesity), known to increase mechanical stress on the spine

Step 4: Apply evidence‐based guidelines for nonspecific low back pain  
– Avoid routine imaging in absence of red flags  
– Maintain or increase activity rather than bed rest  
– Emphasize conservative measures and lifestyle modification, especially weight loss

Answer: Recommend beginning a weight loss program.  
\boxed{D}

Step 1: Characterize the murmur  
– It is a systolic murmur heard best at the base (aortic area) with a palpable thrill.  
Step 2: Analyze the effects of maneuvers  
– Valsalva (↓preload) → murmur ↓  
– Handgrip (↑afterload) → murmur ↓  
– Squatting (↑preload, ↑afterload) → murmur ↑  
Step 3: Recall typical murmur responses  
– Aortic stenosis (AS):  
 • Louder with ↑preload/afterload (squatting)  
 • Softer with ↓preload (Valsalva) or ↑afterload (handgrip)  
– HCM (hypertrophic cardiomyopathy): opposite pattern (murmur ↑ with Valsalva, ↓ with squatting)  
– Mitral regurgitation: murmur ↑ with handgrip  
– Mitral stenosis, aortic insufficiency: diastolic murmurs or different maneuver effects  
Step 4: Match the pattern  
The murmur’s behavior (↓ with Valsalva and handgrip; ↑ with squatting) and its location at the base with a thrill strongly point to aortic stenosis.  

Final Answer: \boxed{Aortic\ stenosis} (B)

Step 1: Localized rash with sharp cutoff at the wrist creases suggests a contact dermatitis pattern limited to whatever covers the hands and wrists.  
Step 2: Among his exposures, mortuary students routinely wear gloves (usually latex) when handling cadavers.  
Step 3: Chronic exposure to latex can induce a type IV hypersensitivity contact dermatitis, presenting as an erythematous rash confined to the glove-covered area.  
Step 4: Other exposures (detergent, chemicals from motorcycles, farm pesticides, pigeons) would either involve broader distribution or different clinical syndromes.  

Final answer: \boxed{\text{Use of latex gloves}}

Step 1: Characterize the mass  
– Location: confined to the scrotum, separate from the testis (testis itself feels normal).  
– Consistency: soft, nontender.  
– Size: ~6 cm, present since childhood.  
– Transillumination: positive (glows with light), indicating fluid content.  
– Auscultation: no bowel sounds in the mass (rules out herniated bowel).

Step 2: Narrow the differential  
– Inguinal hernia: would often have bowel sounds and may be reducible; usually not transilluminant.  
– Varicocele (dilation of pampiniform plexus): feels like a “bag of worms,” typically non-transilluminant.  
– Spermatocele: a cystic dilation of the epididymal ducts, smaller and usually located above the testis.  
– Hydrocele: fluid collection around the testis in the tunica vaginalis, transilluminates.  

Step 3: Distinguish congenital versus acquired hydrocele  
– A congenital (communicating) hydrocele persists from childhood, due to a patent processus vaginalis allowing peritoneal fluid to pass into the scrotal sac.  
– An acquired (noncommunicating) hydrocele arises later in life (trauma, infection, neoplasm).

Step 4: Match to answer choices  
(A) Accumulation of scrotal adipose tissue → would not transilluminate.  
(B) Cryptorchidism of the left testis → undescended testis, not a fluid‐filled mass.  
(C) Dilation of the pampiniform plexus of veins → varicocele (“bag of worms”), non-transilluminant.  
(D) Persistence of a patent processus vaginalis → congenital hydrocele, transilluminant.  
(E) Torsion of the left testis → acute, painful, ischemic testis, not a chronic, painless fluid collection.

Final Answer: \boxed{D}

Step 1: Identify the diagnosis and context  
– The patient’s work‐up and exam are consistent with psychogenic (conversion) blindness, a functional neurologic symptom disorder.  

Step 2: Recognize the proposed intervention  
– The medical student suggests giving a placebo saline injection under the pretense of a “new medication,” with the goal of “temporarily recovering” vision to confirm the absence of true blindness.  

Step 3: Apply ethical principles  
– Beneficence and nonmaleficence: A saline injection is unlikely to benefit the patient medically and exposes him to the (albeit small) risks of an injection (pain, infection).  
– Autonomy and informed consent: Deception violates the patient’s right to make an informed choice. Patients must be given truthful information about any intervention.  
– Veracity: Physicians have an ethical duty to tell the truth; deliberately misleading the patient is not permitted.  

Step 4: Choose the best answer  
– While the student’s intent might be to help (beneficence), the method relies on deception. The principal ethical violation is a breach of veracity (truth‐telling) and informed consent.  

Final Answer: \boxed{D}

Step 1: Identify the object and its location  
– The patient has ingested a 4 cm metallic nail.  
– Abdominal X-ray localizes it to the left upper quadrant (stomach region).  
– No signs of perforation (no free air), obstruction, or peritonitis; patient is asymptomatic.

Step 2: Recall guidelines for ingested foreign bodies  
– Sharp or pointed objects (e.g., nails, pins) carry a high risk of bowel perforation.  
– Any sharp object in the esophagus, stomach, or proximal duodenum should be removed endoscopically if reachable.  
– If a sharp object has passed beyond the reach of the endoscope but does not progress on serial imaging or the patient develops symptoms of perforation, surgical intervention may be required.  
– Cathartics, ipecac, or purely observational approaches risk mucosal injury, obstruction, or perforation with sharp objects and are contraindicated.

Step 3: Apply guidelines to this case  
– The nail is sharp and still within endoscopic reach (stomach).  
– No evidence of perforation yet, so open surgery is not indicated immediately.  
– The safest, least invasive method is endoscopic removal.

Final Answer:  Remove the nail via endoscopic esophagogastroscopy.  

\boxed{E}

Step 1: Identify the clinical context  
– A patient with end‐stage renal disease on hemodialysis is at high risk for anemia, because the kidney normally produces erythropoietin (EPO). Loss of renal function → ↓EPO → normocytic, normochromic anemia with low reticulocyte count.

Step 2: Match the described drug actions  
– “Induces reticulocyte release from the bone marrow” = stimulates erythroid precursors → ↑reticulocytes.  
– “Stimulates a cytokine receptor that signals via the JAK/STAT pathway” = the EPO receptor is a type I cytokine receptor using JAK2/STAT5 signaling.

Step 3: Review answer choices  
(A) Erythropoietin – recombinant EPO analog; acts on EPO receptor (JAK/STAT), ↑reticulocyte production in anemia of chronic kidney disease  
(B) Filgrastim – G-CSF; ↑neutrophil production  
(C) Interleukin-2 – T-cell growth factor; not used to treat anemia  
(D) Platelet-derived growth factor – acts on fibroblast proliferation, not erythropoiesis  
(E) Sargramostim – GM-CSF; ↑granulocytes and macrophages, not specific for red cells

Step 4: Select the best answer  
The drug used to correct anemia in chronic renal failure by stimulating the EPO receptor via JAK/STAT is recombinant erythropoietin.

Final Answer: \boxed{Erythropoietin}

Step 1: Recognize impairment and immediate patient‐safety risk  
– Any clinician working while intoxicated poses a serious threat to patient care.  

Step 2: Remove the impaired clinician from clinical duties  
– You must prevent further patient exposure to an impaired provider.  

Step 3: Notify the appropriate hospital authority  
– Most institutions have a Patient Safety or Risk Management officer (or Medical Director) who must be informed so that formal processes (including fitness‐for‐duty evaluation, reporting, and support programs) can be initiated.  

Other options (simply asking if he’s sober, sending him home without reporting, requesting a BAC, or letting him sleep) fail to ensure patient safety and to trigger the required institutional response.  

Final answer: \boxed{Relieve\ the\ physician\ of\ duty\ and\ alert\ the\ hospital’s\ patient\ safety\ officer\ (C)}.

– Countertransference: the clinician’s emotional reaction to the patient (irrelevant here).  
– Projection: attributing one’s own unacceptable thoughts or feelings to someone else (“I don’t hate her; she hates me”).  
– Projective identification: projecting unwanted parts of the self into another person and behaving in ways that induce the other to feel/act accordingly (more complex, seen in borderline personality).  
– Reaction formation: adopting attitudes opposite to one’s true unacceptable impulses (e.g., overly polite to disguise anger).  
– Splitting: viewing people or situations in “all good” or “all bad” terms, with rapid shifts between extremes.

Step 2: Synthesis  
These findings are classic for complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS): testicular secretion of anti‐Müllerian hormone causes Müllerian duct regression (no uterus), peripheral aromatization of androgens yields normal breast development, and unresponsive androgen receptors yield a female phenotype despite an XY karyotype and high testosterone.

– Gonads (undescended testes) carry a risk of malignancy (gonadoblastoma, seminoma) that rises with age  
– To allow for spontaneous puberty (driven by aromatized estrogens) one delays gonadectomy until after completion of puberty  
– After gonadectomy, patients require lifelong estrogen replacement; progestins are unnecessary because there is no uterus

– She is SMR IV at age 15 (nearly finished with puberty)  
– The next best step is to remove the gonads to eliminate the future malignancy risk

– Factor V Leiden mutation: most common inherited cause of hypercoagulability in Caucasians; causes activated protein C resistance.

Step 1: Identify the finding  
– The patient is asymptomatic but her urine dipstick is positive for leukocyte esterase, microscopic urinalysis shows WBCs, and Gram stain shows gram‐negative rods.  This is consistent with asymptomatic bacteriuria.  

Step 2: Why this matters in pregnancy  
– Asymptomatic bacteriuria occurs in 2–7% of pregnant women.  If left untreated, it can progress to pyelonephritis (up to 30–40% risk) and is associated with adverse outcomes (low birth weight, preterm delivery).  Therefore, all pregnant women with confirmed bacteriuria should be treated, even if they have no symptoms.  

Step 3: Diagnostic confirmation  
– The gold standard is a quantitative urine culture (≥10^5 CFU/mL of a single organism).  In practice, a positive dipstick/UA plus Gram stain often prompts treatment in pregnancy if culture confirmation isn’t separately listed.  

Step 4: Treatment  
– First‐line agents for asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy include nitrofurantoin (except near term) or a β‐lactam such as amoxicillin (if the organism is susceptible).  Metronidazole is for anaerobes/Trichomonas and not indicated here.  Intravenous therapy and imaging (pyelography, cystoscopy) are reserved for complicated or upper tract infections.  Cranberry juice has not been shown to reliably eradicate bacteriuria.  

Answer: Oral amoxicillin therapy  

Final answer: \boxed{Oral\ amoxicillin\ therapy}

– The infraorbital groove runs along the orbital floor inside the orbit, then exits the maxilla as the infraorbital foramen on the anterior face.

Step 1: Identify the key clinical features  
– 10-month history of wide, red striae over the lower trunk  
– “Moon” facies and central (abdominal) obesity with thinning of the extremities  
– ↑Appetite  

These findings are classic for Cushing syndrome (chronic cortisol excess).

Step 2: Recall the effects of cortisol excess  
– Gluconeogenesis → hyperglycemia  
– Protein catabolism → muscle wasting/weakness, thin skin, easy bruising  
– Mineralocorticoid activity → sodium retention, potassium loss, hypertension  
– Redistribution of fat to face, trunk, supraclavicular area (“buffalo hump”), dorsocervical fat pads  

Step 3: Match the expected additional findings to the answer choices  
(A) Hyperkalemia and numerous bruises  
    – Bruises fit, but cortisol excess causes hypokalemia (not hyperkalemia) via mineralocorticoid effects.  
(B) Hypertension and muscle weakness  
    – Both are direct consequences of cortisol excess (sodium retention → hypertension; protein catabolism → muscle weakness).  
(C) Hypocalcemia and hyperglycemia  
    – Hyperglycemia fits, but cortisol does not typically cause hypocalcemia.  
(D) Hypoglycemia and fat pads between the scapulae  
    – It causes hyperglycemia, not hypoglycemia; “buffalo hump” (fat pad) is true but the first part is wrong.  
(E) Jaundice and thinning of the skin  
    – Thinning of skin fits, but jaundice is not a feature of Cushing syndrome.  

Step 4: Select the best answer  
The most consistent additional findings in Cushing syndrome are hypertension (from mineralocorticoid activity) and muscle weakness (from protein catabolism).

Final Answer: \boxed{Hypertension\;and\;muscle\;weakness\;(B)}

Step 1: Identify key features  
– 38-year-old woman on an ACE inhibitor for 6 weeks  
– Recurrent, non-pruritic swelling of face/lips/tongue (angioedema)  
– No urticaria or itching (makes histamine‐mediated allergy unlikely)  
– ACE inhibitors commonly cause angioedema (can occur weeks to months after starting)

Step 2: Recall mechanism of ACE‐inhibitor angioedema  
– ACE (kininase II) normally degrades bradykinin  
– Inhibition of ACE → accumulation of bradykinin → increased vascular permeability → angioedema  

Step 3: Match mediator to presentation  
– Bradykinin: causes non-pruritic angioedema in ACE‐inhibitor use  
– Histamine: would cause itching and urticaria  
– Leukotriene B4: chemotactic factor, not primary mediator of angioedema  
– Nitric oxide/Prostaglandin E2: vasodilators but not the key mediator here  

Final answer:  
\boxed{Bradykinin}

Step 1: Recall the action of ACE inhibitors on renal arterioles  
- ACE inhibitors block formation of angiotensin II.  
- Angiotensin II normally constricts both afferent and (preferentially) efferent arterioles, with a greater effect on the efferent.  

Step 2: Predict arteriolar resistance changes when angiotensin II is decreased  
- With less angiotensin II, both arterioles dilate (resistance ↓), but the efferent arteriole dilates more than the afferent.  

Step 3: Predict change in glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure  
- Dilation of the efferent arteriole (↓ resistance) lowers the hydrostatic pressure in the glomerular capillaries.  
- A smaller drop in afferent resistance has little opposing effect, so net glomerular pressure ↓.  

Step 4: Match to answer choices (showing: afferent resistance, efferent resistance, glomerular hydrostatic pressure)  
(A) ↑  ↑  ↓  
(B) ↑  ↓  ↑  
(C) ↑  ↓  ↓  
(D) ↓  ↑  ↓  
(E) ↓  ↓  ↑  
(F) ↓  ↓  ↓  

ACE inhibition → afferent↓, efferent↓ (more), PGC↓, which corresponds to choice F.  

Final Answer: \boxed{F}

Step 1: Recall the role of cardiac neural crest cells  
- Cardiac neural crest cells migrate into the outflow tract (conotruncal region) of the developing heart and contribute to formation of the aorticopulmonary septum, which divides the truncus arteriosus into the ascending aorta and pulmonary trunk.

Step 2: Predict the consequence of absent cardiac neural crest cells  
- Without these cells, the aorticopulmonary (conotruncal) septum fails to form properly, so the truncus arteriosus remains undivided and gives rise to a single great vessel overlying both ventricles.

Step 3: Match to the listed congenital defects  
- Persistent truncus arteriosus is characterized by a single arterial trunk with a large ventricular septal defect, due to failure of the conotruncal septum to develop.  

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Persistent truncus arteriosus}} (Choice D)

Step 1: Identify the key pathophysiology  
– Pulmonary embolism acutely raises pulmonary vascular resistance → acute pulmonary hypertension → right ventricular (RV) pressure overload.

Step 2: Review the physical signs and their specificity for pulmonary arterial hypertension  
(A) Increased jugular venous pressure  
  – Indicates elevated right atrial (RA) pressure, but can occur in many forms of right‐sided heart failure or fluid overload.  
(B) P2 louder than A2  
  – The pulmonary valve (P2) closure becomes forceful and accentuated when pulmonary artery pressures rise.  A loud P2 is the classic, most specific auscultatory sign of pulmonary hypertension.  
(C) Peripheral edema  
  – Reflects chronic or severe right‐sided failure but is nonspecific (can be seen in renal disease, liver disease, venous insufficiency).  
(D) Presence of an S3  
  – May indicate volume overload and reduced ventricular compliance; an RV S3 can occur but is not as specific for pulmonary hypertension.  
(E) Pulmonary crackles  
  – Suggest left‐sided congestion and pulmonary edema, not pulmonary arterial hypertension per se.

Step 3: Select the most specific sign  
– Among the choices, an accentuated pulmonary component of the second heart sound (P2 > A2) is the most specific physical finding of elevated pulmonary artery pressure.

Final Answer: \boxed{(B)}

Step 1: Identify the contraction pattern  
– Contractions every 2 minutes, lasting 60 seconds → 5 contractions in 10 minutes, each of unusually long duration → uterine tachysystole (hyperstimulation).

Step 2: Describe the fetal heart tracing  
(Although the image is not shown here, the vignette implies “recurrent late decelerations” – gradual decreases in fetal heart rate that begin after the peak of each contraction and recover after the contraction ends.)

Step 3: Link the findings  
– Uterine hyperstimulation reduces the interval for uterine relaxation and placental reperfusion between contractions.  
– This leads to uteroplacental insufficiency during each contraction and causes late decelerations.

Step 4: Exclude the other choices  
– Occult shoulder dystocia: would not produce late decelerations; manifests at delivery.  
– Cord prolapse: produces sudden, deep variable or prolonged decelerations, not repetitive late decels.  
– Uterine rupture: would present with loss of contractions, maternal pain, bleeding, possible loss of station.  
– Chronic uteroplacental insufficiency (e.g., preeclampsia) causes gradual decompensation, not acute onset in the context of tachysystole.

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Uterine hyperstimulation (tachysystole) causing late decelerations.}}

Step 1: Identify key clinical findings  
- 2 months of fatigue and muscle weakness

- Physical: brisk reflexes  
- Labs: K⁺ 3.3 mEq/L (low), Hgb 12 g/dL, Hct 35%  

Step 2: Interpret orthostatic vitals  
- A drop of ≥20 mm Hg systolic or ≥10 mm Hg diastolic defines orthostatic hypotension  
- This patient’s drop is only 6 mm Hg systolic and 1 mm Hg diastolic → not orthostatic hypotension

- Hypokalemia (K⁺ 3.3) causes muscle weakness and fatigue

Step 4: Consider causes of hypokalemia in context  
- Thiazide diuretics increase renal K⁺ loss → hypokalemia  
- Alcohol intoxication does not typically cause isolated hypokalemia with brisk reflexes  
- Dehydration would show more pronounced orthostatic changes  
- Iron deficiency anemia would present with microcytic anemia and does not explain K⁺ loss  

Conclusion: The most likely cause is hypokalemia from chronic thiazide use—an adverse drug effect.  

Final Answer: \boxed{A}

Step 1: Characterize the thrombocytopenia

Step 1: Identify the key clinical features  
– Young adult with fever, malaise, decreased appetite for 6 days

Step 2: Recall the classic causes of parotitis  
– Mumps virus (a Paramyxovirus) is the classic cause of viral parotitis in an unvaccinated or partly vaccinated adolescent/young adult.  
– Epstein–Barr virus causes infectious mononucleosis (pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy), not typically parotitis.  
– Hepatitis B virus causes hepatitis, not salivary gland swelling.  
– Measles virus causes koplik spots, rash, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis—not isolated parotitis.  
– Rubella virus causes a mild rash and lymphadenopathy, but not prominent parotid swelling.

The presentation of tender bilateral parotid gland swelling with systemic symptoms in a college student is pathognomonic for mumps virus.

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Mumps virus (choice D)}}

– Reassurance that it will “resolve in time” without intervention won’t help her cope with the phobia itself.

Step 1: Define fasting states  
- The absorptive (“fed”) state lasts roughly 4 hours after a meal, during which dietary glucose is used and excess stored.  
- The postabsorptive (“early fasting”) state spans about 4–16 hours after eating; hepatic glycogenolysis is the main source of blood glucose.  
- Beyond ~16 hours, the “late fasting” (starvation) state relies increasingly on gluconeogenesis (from amino acids, glycerol, lactate).

Step 2: Time point in question  
The study measures glucose 12 hours after an 800-calorie mixed meal. This falls squarely in the postabsorptive (early fasting) period.

Step 3: Possible mechanisms  
(A) Gut absorption: essentially complete by 4 hours, so not at 12 hours.  
(B) Skeletal muscle release: muscle lacks glucose-6-phosphatase and cannot export free glucose.  
(C) Hepatic glycogenolysis: the primary source of blood glucose 4–16 hours after a meal.  
(D) Increased leptin: leptin regulates appetite and energy expenditure over days, not minute-to-minute glucose homeostasis.  
(E) Inhibition of glucagon: in fasting, glucagon is elevated to promote glucose release, not inhibited.

Step 4: Conclusion  
At 12 hours post-meal, maintenance of normal serum glucose depends mainly on breakdown of liver glycogen.

Final Answer: \boxed{Glycogenolysis\ in\ the\ liver\ (C)}

– Ischemic colitis (more subacute, lower-quadrant pain, bloody diarrhea)  
– Graft leak or aneurysm recurrence would cause hypotension/pulsatile mass  
– Bowel obstruction/ileus less likely to produce sudden severe pain out of proportion  

Step 3: Diagnostic test of choice  
The gold standard for suspected acute mesenteric ischemia is mesenteric (abdominal) arteriography.  

Answer: \boxed{A}

Step 1: Recognize the patient’s problem  
The patient with Parkinson disease is on carbidopa–levodopa but still has “freezing” episodes and motor fluctuations. This suggests diminishing efficacy (“wearing off”) of levodopa over time.

Step 2: Review levodopa metabolism and adjunct options  
- Levodopa is converted to dopamine by aromatic l-amino acid decarboxylase (AADC).  
- Carbidopa inhibits peripheral AADC, increasing cerebral levodopa availability.  
- Other metabolic pathways degrade dopamine or levodopa:  
  • Catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) methylates levodopa/dopamine peripherally and centrally (inhibited by entacapone, tolcapone).  
  • Monoamine oxidase B (MAO-B) oxidatively deaminates dopamine in the brain (inhibited by selegiline, rasagiline).

Step 3: Choose the right adjunct enzyme inhibitor  
Since the patient already takes carbidopa (an AADC inhibitor), further boosting central dopamine levels can be achieved by slowing its breakdown in the brain. Inhibiting MAO-B decreases intraneuronal dopamine catabolism, prolonging dopamine action and reducing “off” periods.

Step 4: Match to answer choices  
(A) AADC = already inhibited by carbidopa  
(B) Dopamine β-hydroxylase = converts dopamine → norepinephrine, not targeted in PD  
(C) MAO-B = the correct target for adjunct therapy (e.g., selegiline)  
(D) Phenylethanolamine N-methyltransferase = converts norepinephrine → epinephrine  
(E) Tyrosine hydroxylase = rate-limiting step for catecholamine synthesis, not therapeutically inhibited

Final Answer: \boxed{C}
